chapters.title: Force Is An Interaction
chapters.learningOutcome: Identify the two objects in a force interaction and distinguish contact from non-contact forces.

narration.title: Watch the motion
narration.text: Watch these two skaters. Their rolling platforms are still. They press their palms together, and both begin to glide apart. Here is the mystery: where did the force come from? Do not answer with a single object. Look for a pair.

narration.title: A force needs two
narration.text: A force is a push or pull in an interaction between two objects. The first skater pushes on the second skater. At the same time, the second skater pushes on the first. One interaction, two objects, and a force on each object.

narration.title: Name both objects
narration.text: Our arrows must tell the whole story. This gold arrow means skater A on skater B. The matching arrow means skater B on skater A. The arrows point in opposite directions because each skater is pushed away from the contact.

narration.title: Motion is evidence
narration.text: Now compare before and after. Before contact, both platforms were still. After the push, the skaters move apart. That change in motion is evidence that forces acted. We did not see a force by itself; we saw what the forces changed.

narration.title: The push has ended
narration.text: Here is a common trap: saying the push is stored inside a skater and rides along. It does not. Once their hands separate, that hand-to-hand force has ended. The skaters keep moving for a while, but the contact push is no longer acting.

narration.title: Push or pull
narration.text: Contact forces can push or pull. A hand pushes a cart. A rope pulls a trolley. In each case, name both objects: hand on cart, or rope on trolley. If you can name only one object, your explanation is not finished.

narration.title: No touch required
narration.text: Some forces act without the objects touching. These magnet carts have a clear air gap, yet their motion can change. The interaction is magnet on magnet. Gravity is another non-contact interaction: Earth pulls on a book even when the book is falling through the air.

narration.title: Contact or non-contact
narration.text: Use two questions. Which two objects interact? Do they touch? Foot on ball is contact. Magnet on magnet can be non-contact. Earth on ball is non-contact. The label comes from the evidence, not from whether the force looks strong or weak.

narration.title: Test one change
narration.text: Suppose we want to compare two pushes. Use the same cart and the same surface, then change only the push. Measure how far the cart travels. If we change the cart, surface and push together, the result cannot tell us which change mattered.

narration.title: Repair the explanation
narration.text: Try this explanation: the ball moved because it had force inside it. Repair it. A foot pushed on the ball during contact, so the ball's motion changed. After the foot left, that contact force ended. Exact object pairs make the explanation stronger.

narration.title: Predict the evidence
narration.text: Final challenge. Two magnet carts begin still with a gap between them. They move apart. Which evidence supports a non-contact push? The gap stayed visible, both motions changed, and no hand touched either cart. That is a claim supported by observations.

narration.title: Your physics move
narration.text: Your Physics move is simple and powerful: name the two objects, decide whether they touch, then use the change in motion as evidence. Force is not a substance hiding inside an object. Force is an interaction. You are ready for the cockpit check.

tests.prompt: Two skaters press palms and move apart. Which statement names the interaction most completely?
options: Skater A has force inside
Skater A pushes skater B, and skater B pushes skater A
The floor gives both skaters stored motion
Only the heavier skater pushes
tests.feedback: A force interaction involves two objects. Each skater pushes on the other during contact.

tests.prompt: A foot kicks a still ball. Which object pair belongs in the contact-force explanation?
options: Foot and ball
Ball and goal
Ball and its stored force
Foot and the air only
tests.feedback: The foot and ball touch during the kick, so they are the relevant interaction pair.

tests.prompt: Skater A is on the left and pushes Skater B to the right. Which way should the arrow labelled 'A on B' point?
options: Left
Right
Up
It should have no direction
tests.feedback: The arrow shows the direction of the force on B, so it points to the right.

tests.prompt: A hand pulls a trolley left with a rope. Which arrow best represents 'rope on trolley'?
options: An arrow left from the trolley
An arrow right from the trolley
An arrow down from the rope
No arrow because pulling is not a force
tests.feedback: The rope pulls the trolley left, so the force arrow on the trolley points left.

tests.prompt: Both skaters are still before their palms meet and moving apart afterwards. What is the strongest evidence that forces acted?
options: Their clothes have different colours
Their motion changed after the contact
The platforms have wheels
The room is bright
tests.feedback: A change from still to moving is observable evidence that forces affected the skaters' motion.

tests.prompt: A cart is still, then speeds up while a hand pushes it. Which two observations form useful before-and-after evidence?
options: Cart colour and wheel colour
Room temperature and bench length
Still before and speeding up during the push
Hand size and cart brand
tests.feedback: Comparing motion before and during the push links the interaction to a visible change.

tests.prompt: The skaters' hands separate, but they continue gliding. What happened to the hand-to-hand push?
options: It is stored inside them
It became gravity
It ended when contact ended
It became stronger
tests.feedback: The hand-to-hand contact force ends when the hands separate; continuing motion does not mean the push is stored.

tests.prompt: A foot leaves a kicked ball. Which explanation is most accurate?
options: The foot's push continues inside the ball
The foot-ball force ended when contact ended
No force acted at any time
The ball now creates its own kick
tests.feedback: The foot's contact force acts during contact and ends when the foot leaves the ball.

tests.prompt: Two magnet carts begin still with a visible gap and then move apart. Which conclusion fits the evidence?
options: A contact push acted through the track
A non-contact magnetic interaction changed their motion
The carts stored force before the test
No force acted because they did not touch
tests.feedback: The gap remains visible while motion changes, supporting a non-contact magnetic interaction.

tests.prompt: A book falls after being released. Which interaction can act without contact between the book and the ground?
options: Ground on book
Hand on book
Earth on book
Table on book
tests.feedback: Earth's gravitational pull on the book is a non-contact interaction.

tests.prompt: A rope moves a trolley towards a child. Which description is complete?
options: The trolley moves by itself
The rope pulls on the trolley during contact
The child stores force in the trolley
Gravity pulls the trolley towards the child
tests.feedback: The rope and trolley interact through contact, and the rope pulls the trolley.

tests.prompt: A hand moves a cart away from the child. Which words best describe the interaction?
options: Hand pushes cart
Cart pulls Earth
Cart stores hand force
Air pushes hand
tests.feedback: The hand is in contact with the cart and pushes it away.

tests.prompt: A student compares a gentle and strong push. Which plan gives the clearest evidence?
options: Change the cart, surface and push together
Use the same cart and surface, change only the push, and measure distance
Use two carts and guess which moved faster
Change only the surface and call it a push test
tests.feedback: Keeping the cart and surface the same isolates the push as the changed variable.

tests.prompt: Two carts travel different distances, but one ran on carpet and one on tile. Why can this test not compare push strength fairly?
options: Distance cannot be measured
The surfaces also changed
Carts cannot feel forces
A strong push never changes motion
tests.feedback: Changing the surface as well as the push creates two possible causes for the distance difference.

tests.prompt: Which claim contains the 'force is stored inside' misconception?
options: A hand pushes a cart during contact
A magnet can push another magnet across a gap
The kick's force rides inside the ball after contact
Earth pulls on a falling book
tests.feedback: A force is an interaction; the foot-ball contact force does not ride inside the ball.

tests.prompt: A student says, 'No force can act unless objects touch.' Which observation best challenges the claim?
options: A hand pushes a cart
A rope pulls a trolley
Separated magnet carts move apart
A shoe presses the floor
tests.feedback: The visible gap and changing motion show that a magnetic force can act without contact.

tests.prompt: A ball changes from still to moving when kicked. Which reasoning chain is strongest?
options: The ball moved, so it must contain force
Foot and ball touched; the foot pushed the ball; the ball's motion changed
The goal pulled the ball forwards
The ball moved because moving is natural
tests.feedback: The answer names the interacting objects, the push and the observed change in motion.

tests.prompt: Two magnets do not touch, yet one cart starts moving. Which three-part explanation is best?
options: There was a gap; magnetic interaction acted; motion changed
The cart had wheels; wheels contain force; motion appeared
The bench pushed sideways; the magnet did nothing
No contact means no evidence
tests.feedback: The explanation links the visible gap, a non-contact interaction and the change in motion.

tests.prompt: A diagram shows only one arrow labelled 'push' between two skaters. What is the most useful improvement?
options: Make the arrow decorative
Add the two object names and show the matching opposite interaction
Remove all arrows because forces are invisible
Label the arrow 'speed'
tests.feedback: Object-pair labels and the matching interaction make the force model precise.

tests.prompt: Which final explanation uses claim and evidence most effectively?
options: A force happened because I think so
The magnets repelled because they are blue
A non-contact magnetic push acted because the carts moved apart while a gap remained
The carts moved because the track was long
tests.feedback: The claim names the force type and the evidence identifies the visible gap and motion change.

chapters.title: Motion Tells The Story
chapters.learningOutcome: Use before-and-after evidence to explain changes in motion.

chapters.title: Push, Pull And Support
chapters.learningOutcome: Model applied and support forces with a comparison meter.

chapters.title: Friction Grips
chapters.learningOutcome: Explain how friction can help or hinder motion.

chapters.title: Gravity Pulls
chapters.learningOutcome: Explain Earth's non-contact pull on objects.

chapters.title: Magnets Push And Pull
chapters.learningOutcome: Predict magnetic attraction and repulsion using evidence.

chapters.title: Forces Team Up
chapters.learningOutcome: Use several qualitative arrows to predict motion.

chapters.title: Air Pushes Back
chapters.learningOutcome: Investigate how shape changes air resistance.

chapters.title: Machines Trade Force For Distance
chapters.learningOutcome: Explain how ramps and levers trade force for distance.

chapters.title: The Broken Experiment
chapters.learningOutcome: Diagnose unfair tests and strengthen conclusions.

chapters.title: Rescue The Rover
chapters.learningOutcome: Use force evidence to choose and justify an engineering solution.
